content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

June 1, 1983


Buck Delventhal
George Agnost
Office of the City Attorney
City Hall, Room 206
San Francisco, CA 94102

Re: Your Request For Written
Advice, Our Advice
No. A-83-123

Dear Mr. Delventhal:

You have made a telephone request for written advice
pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18329. You are seeking a clarification of my
advice letter to Councilmember Hal Bernson (Los Angeles City
Council) regarding receipt of tickets to Raiders games at the
Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum. (Advice Letter No. A-82-211,
Jan. 19, 1983.) Your clients wish to understand the
applicability of the Bernson advice to their circumstances in
San Francisco.

FACTS

The City of San Francisco leases Candlestick Park to the
San Francisco Giants baseball team and to the San Francisco
Forty-Niners football team. Each lease of $1 million or more is
required to be approved by the Board of Supervisors by ordinance
and then must be acted upon by the Mayor (sign or veto, etc.).
Each of the aforementioned leases fits into this category.

In the past, certain high-ranking city officials have
received gift season tickets from both the Giants and the
Forty-Niners. These have had a value in excess of $250. Some
recipients have returned or paid for all or a portion of the
tickets, others have kept them all.

Buck Delventhal
June 1, 1983
Page 2


QUESTION

You have asked the following question:

May the City and County of San Francisco, acting
by and through its Board of Supervisors and Mayor,
approve a stadium lease with the Giants and/or
Forty-Niners, which would reserve to the City or
require the lessee sport teams to provide to the City,
a designated number of seats which the Mayor may
distribute to City officials according to her
discretion?

ANALYSIS

Initially, it may be helpful to review the basic provisions
of the Political Reform Act/ (the "Act") regarding gifts.
The relevant portions of Section 82028, which defines the term
"gift" for purposes of the Act, are set forth below.

82028. "Gift" means, except as provided in
subdivision (b), any payment to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received
and includes a rebate or discount in the price of
anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made
in the regular course of business to members of the
public without regard to official status. Any person,
other than a defendant in a criminal action, who claims
that a payment is not a gift by reason of receipt of
consideration has the burden of proving that the
consideration received was of equal or greater value.

(B). The term "gift" does not include:

* * *

(2) Gifts which are not used and which,
within 30 days after receipt, are returned to the
donor or delivered to a charitable organization
without being claimed as a charitable contribution
for tax purposes;

* * *

_________________________
1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. All
statutory references made are to the Government Code unless
otherwise specified.

Buck Delventhal
June 1, 1983
Page 3


Receipt of a gift or gifts from one donor totaling $50 or
more during any reporting period (usually a calendar year) must
be reported on the official's Statement of Economic Interests
("SEI"). Section 87207(a)(1). Because a gift is "income"
within the meaning of the Act (Section 82030(a)), the donor (or
"source") of a gift or gifts totaling $250 or more in value
during the preceding 12-month period is a financial interest to
the official within the meaning of Section 87103(c). Consequently, an official who has received gifts of $250 or more must
disqualify himself or herself from making or participating in
the making of any governmental decision which will have a
material financial effect on that source of income which will be
distinguishable from the decision's effect upon the public
generally.

In dealing with the Giants and the Forty-Niners, there is no
question that the effect upon either lessee will be distinguishable from the effect upon the public generally. It is
equally clear that the effect will be material solely by
definition, we are only concerned with leases of $1 million or
more. (See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(C), which
refers to 18702(b)(1).) Therefore, if any official has received
$250 or more in tickets within the 12-month period preceding any
lease decision as to that donor, he or she must disqualify as to
that lease decision.2/

A review of the most recent SEI filings by the Mayor and the
Board of Supervisors (for calendar year 1982) reveals the
following gifts from the Giants and the Forty-Niners.

Mayor Diane Feinstein                    None Reported

Supervisor Quentin Kopp                  49ers - $923
                                         Giants - 250

Supervisor Willie Kennedy                49ers - 240
                                         Giants - 240

Supervisor Harry Britt                   49ers - 250
                                         Giants - 250

_________________________

2/ You have advised that, pursuant to the Charter of the
City and County of San Francisco, the Mayor is the only official
who can act on an ordinance adopted by the Board of Supervisors.
Consequently, her participation is "legally required" under Section
87101 and she would not be required to disqualify herself.

Buck Delventhal
June 1, 1983
Page 4


Supervisor Louise Renne3/          49ers - $ 60
                                    Giants - 187

Supervisor Doris Ward              49ers - 250
                                    Giants - 250

Supervisor Richard Hongisto         Giants - 250

Supervisor Carol Ruth Silver        49ers - Less than $250
                                    Giants - Less than $250

Supervisor Wendy Nelder             49ers - 249
                                    Giants - 249

Supervisor Nancy Walker             49ers - 240
                                    Giants - 240

Supervisor John Molinari4/          49ers - 150
                                    Giants - 235


     As can be seen from the foregoing, Supervisors Kopp, Britt
and Ward may have to disqualify themselves from any lease
decisions involving the Forty-Niners (depending on when the
tickets were received and when the decision comes up). These
same three supervisors, plus Supervisor Hongisto, may have to
disqualify themselves as to any lease decision involving the
Giants (again, dependent upon the timing of the gift and the
decision).

     In addition to the 1982 tickets, all or some of the
supervisors may have received gift tickets during 1983 which
(either by themselves or when combined with tickets previously
received in the last twelve months) could form the basis for
disqualification. For instance, Supervisor Molinari's addendum


___________________________

3/  Supervisor Renne attached a sheet reflecting the total
values of tickets received and the amounts returned or paid for,
but it does not indicate if these actions occurred within 30
days of receipt.

4/  Supervisor Molinari attached an addendum reflecting
total value received and value of tickets returned or paid for,
but it does not specify whether the tickets were returned within
30 days of receipt.

Buck Delventhal
June 1, 1983
Page 5


indicates that he received his Giants tickets on April 6, 1982.
Presumably others received their tickets at or about the same
time. Consequently, more than 12 months has passed since
receipt of that gift and it would not be disqualifying even if
it were $250 or more in value. However, it is also likely that
1983 Giants season tickets have recently been received and the
value of the unreturned/unpaid for tickets would determine if a
conflict of interest exists requiring disqualification.

In addition to the value of the tickets received, there must
be added the value of any free parking provided at the stadium
by either the Giants or the Forty-Niners. Unlike the tickets,
which are valued on their face, the free parking is valued on a
reasonable use basis, unless transferrable to others. (See the
Commission's Hopkins Opinion, 3 FPPC Opinions 107, No. 77-022,
Dec. 8, 1977, copy enclosed.)

If enough supervisors must disqualify themselves under
Section 87100 so that a quorum is no longer possible, then a
sufficient number may be permitted to vote as to achieve a
quorum. (See the Commission's Hudson Opinion, 4 FPPC Opinions
13, No. 77-007, Feb. 7, 1978, copy enclosed).

We turn now to the thrust of your question, which we
understand to be whether the City may negotiate to receive the
tickets or retain control of the seats and then have the Mayor
distribute admissions so that the receipt by officials will be
considered as part of the compensation of such officials (as in
the Bernson situation) as opposed to a gift from the private
source (i.e., the Giants or Forty-Niners).

Initially, we point out that successfully transposing the
tickets from being gifts to being compensation could result in
their value becoming taxable income, rather than a gift.2/
However, a successful transposition may not be easily attained
under the circumstances which you postulate.

In the Commission's discussion at the hearing upon its
advice to John Witt, City Attorney for San Diego, it was the
Commission's position that, under the circumstances in San Diego
(where the City retained the possession and control of certain
seats which were utilized by Councilmembers and certain other
high-ranking city officials all of whom had responsibilities
toward the stadium and its operation), the free use of the

5/ Under these circumstances, the tickets would basically
amount to an increase in the pay of the officials receiving them.

Buck Delventhal
June 1, 1983
Page 6


admission to the "City Box" was a "perk" of office and
constituted part of these officials' compensation from the
City. Therefore, the use of this privilege was neither
reportable as a gift nor the basis for a disqualification.

In our advice letter to Councilmember Bernson, we applied
the Commission's reasoning to the unique circumstances
involved. There, the Coliseum Commission had negotiated for a
certain number of complimentary tickets to be distributed by the
Raiders. However, due to unforeseen events, the Coliseum
Commission distributed the tickets, at its discretion. Because
the Commission is a triuvirate, we held that tickets dis-
tributed to high-ranking City, County and State Officials 6/
who have responsibilities toward the Coliseum and its operation,
were part of their compensation from their respective agencies
and, hence, neither reportable nor the basis for disqualification.

In the Bernson situation, the increase in the officials' compen-
sation occurred de facto and, in all but a very few instances, was
not within the control of the officials who received the increase.
Councilmember Bernson did not participate in any way in the nego-
tiations over the tickets. In the situation you postulate, the
Board of Supervisors would be making the decision with foreknowledge
that the consequences of the decision would be to increase the
supervisors' compensation. A decision to increase an official's own
compensation does not require disqualification on that basis.1/
Commission regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(d)(3) states as
follows:

(d) Making or participating in the making of a
governmental decision shall not include:

* * *

(3) Actions by public officials, employees,
or employee representatives relating to their
compensation or the terms or conditions of their
compensation or the terms or conditions of their
employment contract.

_____________

6/ See Advice Letter to Norman Flette, Deputy Attorney
General, also No. A-82-211, following up on Bernson letter, copy
enclosed.

7/ However, as indicated earlier, several of the Supervisors
may be required to disqualify themselves because of their receipt of
gifts equaling $250 or more.

Buck Delventhal
June 1, 1983
Page 7


However, in order for the tickets to qualify as compensation
from the City, they must be distributed according to some
regular plan, not "at the discretion of the Mayor," as you
propose. If the Mayor has the discretion to distribute the
tickets, we would hold the receipt of the tickets to be the
receipt of a gift from the City dispensed by the Mayor. Such a
gift would be reportable if its value equals $50 or more, but
would not form the basis for future disqualification.8/

All of the foregoing discussion assumes that the City, or
the Mayor, does not merely become the agent or intermediary for
distributing gifts from the Giants or Forty-Niners. Under
Section 87210, such a gift would still be attributable to its
original source. The tickets must be acquired by the City
through its lease contract and must become the property of the
City prior to their distribution in order for them to be
construed as compensation, or as gifts, from the City to its
officials.

Should you have any questions, please do not hesitate to
call me. I may be reached at 916/322-5901.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel, Legal Division

REL:km
Enclosures


_________________
8/ On the other hand, the decision to permit the Mayor to
bestow such gifts upon themselves (if receipt of the gift is
"reasonably foreseeable" as to any particular supervisor) would
necessitate disqualification of those supervisors if the value
of the gift will exceed $100. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18720(b)(3)(A).

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660         322-5901         322-6441 • •        322-6444


January 26, 1983


Norman Flette
Deputy Attorney General
Department of Justice
3580 Wilshire Blvd.
Los Angeles, CA  90010

                                        Re: Los Angeles Coliseum - Raiders
                                            Tickets
                                            Our File No. A-82-211

Dear Mr. Flette:

     You have requested a clarification of one portion of our
recent letter to Los Angeles City Councilman Hall Bernson
(No. A-82-211). You are the Deputy Attorney General assigned to
advise the State's participants on the Coliseum Commission. You
have provided the following supplemental facts:

     "The State's portion of the triumvirate Coliseum Commission
("Coliseum") is the 6th Agricultural District (The California
Museum of Science and Industry). The Board of the 6th Agri-
cultural District is appointed by the Governor to fixed terms.
This Board in turn, selects from its own membership its
representatives on the Coliseum.

     I have advised you that, based upon these facts, the
officials of the 6th Agricultural District would be treated the
same as city and county officials.* Thus, the distribution of
free tickets to the Raiders games, by the Coliseum (as described
in the Benson letter) to 6th Agricultural District officials
would be considered part of their compensation and thus not a

_________________________________
*Officials here include all high-ranking city and county
officials, such as treasurer, city attorney, county counsel,
etc.

Norman Fleete
January 26, 1983
Page Two


gift. Contrary to what the Bernson letter stated, this is not
limited to the 6th Agricultural District officials who actually
sit on the Coliseum Commission.

I trust that this clarifies the matter to your satisfaction.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert E. Leidign
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

REL:plh
cc: Anthony Saul Alperin
    Thomas Bonaventura
    Deputy City Attorneys

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • •    Administration • •    Executive/Legal • •    Enforcement • •    Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5562             322-5660              322-5901             322-6441             322-6444


                                        June 10, 1983


Buck Delventhal
George Agnost
Office of the City Attorney
City Hall, Room 206
San Francisco, CA  94102

                                        Re:  Your Request for Written
                                             Advice
                                             Our Advice No. A-83-123
                                             (Follow-Up Letter)

Dear Mr. Delventhal:

     You have made a telephone request for follow-up advice on
our recent letter to you, dated June 1, 1983 (also No.
A-83-123).  Specifically, you have asked about valuation of
nontransferrable free parking passes from the Giants and the
49ers.  Your question has two parts:  First, how should a
recipient supervisor value the pass if he or she desires to keep
the total value of the gift received (tickets plus parking)
below $250; and, secondly, if the gift is received and the
supervisor merely wants to report its value or to reimburse the
donor for all or a portion, now should the pass be valued?

     In the first instance, if the supervisor will be returning
tickets to some of the games in order to reduce the value of the
gift to less than $250, he or she should include a cover letter
with their return in which he or she states that the tickets are
being returned unused within 30 days of their receipt and that
the parking pass will only be used for those games for which
tickets have been retained.  The gift is then the value of the
tickets retained and the value of the parking pass.  The value
of the parking pass is the appropriate value per use multiplied
by the number of games to be attended.

Buck Delventhal
June 10, 1983
Page 2


     In the second instance, where all of the tickets are
retained, the value of the nontransferrable parking pass is its
market value (if known, i.e. if such a pass is sold by the donor
to others) or its value based upon the maximum reasonable use,
as set forth in the Hopkins Opinion, No. 77-022, 3 FPPC Opinions
107, Dec. 8, 1977 (copy enclosed). Since the Hopkins opinion
contains a thorough discussion for such valuations, we will not
elaborate further here.

     Should you have any further questions, do not hesitate to
call me again at (916) 322-5901.

                                          Sincerely,


                                          Robert E. Leidign
                                          Counsel
                                          Legal Division

ADDENDUM NO. 4
STATEMENT OF ECONOMIC INTERESTS - FORM 721


John L. Molinari
Member, Board of Supervisors
City and County of San Francisco




     This Addendum is added to further explain the gifts of
the San Francisco Giants Baseball Club and the San Francisco
Forty Niners reported on Page 11, Schedule F - Gifts.

     I received from the San Francisco Giants Baseball Club
box seat tickets with a total value of $1,015.00 as follows. On
April 6, 1982 I received tickets valued at $960.00; on July 21,
1982 I received tickets valued at $42.00 and on September 18,
1982 I received tickets valued at $13.00. Unused tickets with
a total value of $780.00 were returned. The total value of
tickets used was $235.00.

     I received from the San Francisco Forty Niners reserved
seat tickets with a total value of $300.00 on July 27, 1982. I
paid for two tickets to each of two games with a total value of
$60.00 on August 3, 1982. In addition, there were three games
cancelled due to the National Football League players' strike
and the total value of tickets not used for those games was
$90.00. The total value of tickets used was $150.00.




                                                        Page 9a

State of California

June 10, 1983


Buck Delvennthal
George Agnost
Office of the City Attorney
City Hall, Room 205
San Francisco, CA 94102

Re: Your Request for Written
Advice
Our Advice No. A-83-123
(Follow-Up Letter)

Dear Mr. Delvennthal:

You have made a telephone request for follow-up advice on
our recent letter to you, dated June 1, 1983 (also no.
A-83-123). Specifically, you have asked about valuation of
nontransferable free parking passes from the Giants and the
49ers. Your question has two parts: First, how should a
recipient supervisor value the pass if he or she desires to keep
the total value of the gift received (tickets plus parking)
below $250; and, secondly, if the gift is received and the
supervisor merely wants to report its value or to reimburse the
donor for all or a portion, now should the pass be valued?

In the first instance, if the supervisor will be returning
tickets to some of the games in order to reduce the value of the
gift to less than $250, he or she should include a cover letter
with their return in which he or she states that the tickets are
being returned unused within 30 days of their receipt and that
the parking pass will only be used for those games for which
tickets have been retained. The gift is then the value of the
tickets retained and the value of the parking pass. The value
of the parking pass is the appropriate value per use multiplied
by the number of games to be attended.

Buck Delventhal
June 10, 1983
Page 2


     In the second instance, where all of the tickets are
retained, the value of the nontransferable parking pass is its
market value (if known, i.e. if such a pass is sold by the donor
to others) or its value based upon the maximum reasonable use,
as set forth in the Hopkins Opinion, No. 77-022, 3 FPPC Opinions
40, Dec. 8, 1977 (copy enclosed). Since the Hopkins Opinion
contains a thorough discussion for such valuations, we will not
elaborate further here.

     Should you have any further questions, do not hesitate to
call me again at (916) 322-5901.

                                   Sincerely,



                                   Robert E. Leidign
                                   Counsel
                                   Legal Division

May the City and County of San Francisco, acting 
by and through its Board of Supervisors and Mayor, 
approve a stadium lease with the Giants and/or 
Forty-Niners, which would reserve to the City, or 
require the lessee sport teams to provide to the City, 
a designated number of seats which the Mayor may 
distribute to City officials according to her 
discretion?

Some

sections.analysis:
Initially, it may be help-ful to review the basic provisions 
of the Political Reform Act1:?' (the "Act") regarding gifts. 
The relevant portions of Section 82028, which defines the term 
"gift" for purposes of the Act, are set forth below. 
82028. 
"Gift" means, except as provided in 
sUbdivision (b), any payment to the extent that 
consideration of equal or greater value is not received 
and includes a rebate or discount in the price of 
anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made 
in the regular course of business to members of the 
public without regard to official status. 
Any person, 
other than a defendant in a criminal action, who claims 
that a payment is not a gift by reason of receipt of 
consideration has the burden of proving that the 
consideration receivea was of equal or greater value. 
(b). 
The term "gift" does not include:
sections.question_synthetic: Whether San Francisco can approve stadium leases with the Giants and/or Forty-Niners that reserve seats for the Mayor to distribute to city officials, and whether such distributed tickets would constitute compensation (avoiding gift reporting/disqualification requirements) rather than gifts from private donors.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that while tickets could theoretically qualify as compensation if distributed under a regular plan as part of official duties, the San Francisco scenario differs materially from the Bernson precedent because supervisors would be voting to increase their own compensation with foreknowledge, and distribution would need to follow established procedures tied to official responsibilities to avoid gift characterization and disqualification requirements.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document appears truncated at end. Full CONCLUSION section not present in provided text. Analysis section provides the advisory reasoning and conclusion. Multiple footnotes and prior opinion citations included. Document discusses specific supervisor gift disclosures from 1982 SEI filings.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked the following question: 
May the City and County of San Francisco, acting 
by and through its Board of Supervisors and Mayor, 
approve a stadium lease with the Giants and/or 
Forty-Niners, which would reserve to the City, or 
require the lessee sport teams to provide to the City, 
a designated number of seats which the Mayor may 
distribute to City officials according to her 
discretion?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC concludes that while tickets could theoretically qualify as compensation if distributed under a regular plan as part of official duties, the San Francisco scenario differs materially from the Bernson precedent because supervisors would be voting to increase their own compensation with foreknowledge, and distribution would need to follow established procedures tied to official responsibilities to avoid gift characterization and disqualification requirements.

Adm. Code Section 18329. You are seeking a clarification of my advice letter to Councilmember Hal Bernson (Los Angeles City Council) regarding receipt of tickets to Raiders games at the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum. (Advice Letter No. A-82-211, Jan. 19, 1983.) Your clients wish to understand the applicability of the Bernson advice to their circumstances in San Francisco. FACTS The City of San Francisco leases Candlestick Park to the San Francisco Giants baseball team and to the San Francisco Forty-Niners football team. Each lease of $1 million or more is required to be approved by the Board of Supervisors by ordinance and then must be acted upon by the Mayor (sign or veto, etc.). Each of the aforementioned leases fits into this category. In the past, certain high-ranking city officials have received gift season tickets from both the Giants and the Forty-Niners. These have had a value in excess of $250. Some recipients have returned or paid for all or a portion of the tickets, others have kept them all. Buck Delventhal June 1, 1983 Page 2 QUESTION You have asked the following question: May the City and County of San Francisco, acting by and through its Board of Supervisors and Mayor, approve a stadium lease with the Giants and/or Forty-Niners, which would reserve to the City or require the lessee sport teams to provide to the City, a designated number of seats which the Mayor may distribute to City officials according to her discretion? ANALYSIS Initially, it may be helpful to review the basic provisions of the Political Reform Act/ (the "Act") regarding gifts. The relevant portions of Section 82028, which defines the term "gift" for purposes of the Act, are set forth below. 82028. "Gift" means, except as provided in subdivision (b), any payment to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official status. Any person, other than a defendant in a criminal action, who claims that a payment is not a gift by reason of receipt of consideration has the burden of proving that the consideration received was of equal or greater value. (B). The term "gift" does not include: * * * (2) Gifts which are not used and which, within
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter addressing whether San Francisco officials can receive stadium season tickets as compensation rather than gifts by negotiating lease terms with the Giants and Forty-Niners, analyzing disqualification requirements and distinguishing the circumstances from prior Bernson precedent.
